title: Boundary-Aware QFT Block-Encoding of Fractional Laplacians
abstract: We study the quantum Fourier transform (QFT) block-encoding of the semi-discrete fractional Laplacian on bounded domains with open, zero-extension boundary conditions. In the notation of the main construction, the target operator is the finite Toeplitz truncation \(A^{(N)}_{α,h}\) obtained from the full-lattice semi-discrete operator with symbol \(|ξ|^α\). A finite QFT register, however, diagonalizes circulant matrices rather than Toeplitz truncations. The native QFT circuit therefore implements a periodic surrogate \(\widetilde A^{(N)}_{α,h}\), not the open-boundary operator. We identify this mismatch through an exact Toeplitz-to-circulant aliasing identity.   To recover the open-boundary action, we zero-pad the state into a larger \(M\)-point QFT register, apply the same Fourier-symbol block-encoding, and compress back to the physical subspace. The resulting compressed block satisfies \(P_{N\to M}^{\dagger}\widetilde A^{(M)}_{α,h}P_{N\to M} = A^{(N)}_{α,h}+E^{(M)}\), where \(E^{(M)}\) is controlled by the tail of the semi-discrete convolution kernel. Thus, the QFT layer implements the fractional symbol, while zero-padding supplies the open-boundary geometry. The construction is an operator-compilation primitive for boundary-aware quantum simulation rather than a complete PDE solver.
summary: This paper provides a way to use the Quantum Fourier Transform to simulate fractional diffusion processes on bounded domains without the errors caused by periodic wrap-around. By padding the quantum register with zeros, the authors show how to effectively implement open boundary conditions for the fractional Laplacian. This serves as a fundamental building block for more complex quantum algorithms for solving partial differential equations.
main_problem: Standard Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) circuits naturally implement periodic boundary conditions, which introduces artificial 'wrap-around' aliasing errors when trying to simulate the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains with open boundaries.
main_result: The authors prove that by zero-padding the input state into a larger M-point QFT register and then compressing it back, one can implement the open-boundary Toeplitz operator with a controllable error that vanishes as the padding increases.
method: The method uses a padding-and-compression mechanism combined with a block-encoding of the Fourier symbol via a diagonal spectral oracle and controlled Ry rotations.
model_or_system: The semi-discrete fractional Laplacian operator (with symbol |xi|^alpha) on bounded domains with open, zero-extension boundary conditions.
key_observables: spectral norm of the error matrix E(M), gate complexity, and the decay of the convolution kernel tail.
important_parameters: fractional order alpha, grid spacing h, physical dimension N, padded dimension M, and the decay rate r_alpha = min(2, 1 + alpha).
main_assumptions: The method assumes a sufficiently large padding (M >= 2N) and relies on the specific decay properties of the kernel coefficients to bound the error.
figures_summary: Figure 4 compares the matrix structures of Open Toeplitz vs. QFT circulant operators highlighting aliasing; Figure 5 shows the functional accuracy of the padded method compared to the target operator.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the boundary mismatch problem, provides a mathematical proof of the aliasing identity, introduces the padding-and-compression construction, performs error analysis using the Schur test, and demonstrates a concrete quantum oracle implementation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustrative four-qubit QFT decomposition. The finite QFT is the spectral layer used by the block-encoding, but by itself it imposes periodic rather than open geometry.
FIG. 2. Native QFT block-encoding of the periodic circulant surrogate e A(N) α,h. The frequency oracle encodes the fractional symbol as an ancilla amplitude.
FIG. 3. Compressed open-boundary block from zero-padding. The size-M QFT circuit is periodic on the padded register, while compression back to the first N coordinates gives (A(N) α,h + E(M))/λmax.
FIG. 4. Matrix structure for N = 64, h = 1, and α = 1.5. The third panel shows the absolute aliasing error on a logarithmic color scale; the highlighted corners are the wrap-around regions.

Figure 5 gives a direct functional comparison using three bounded-domain test functions also used in Ref. [19]. We compare the open-boundary Toeplitz target, the native QFT-circulant surrogate, and the zero-padded compressed operator. The top row overlays the operator outputs, while the bottom row shows the pointwise absolute error on a logarithmic scale. The native QFT curve plays the role of a periodic Fourier method and displays boundary artifacts, whereas the padded construction follows the open-boundary target.
